preamble.summary: Targum Onkelos on Leviticus addresses the sacrificial and purity systems with attention to anti-anthropomorphism (God does not 'smell' offerings), Shekinah theology (the divine presence sanctifies and inhabits the Tabernacle), and halakhic clarification. Because Leviticus is primarily legal material, Onkelos' interpretive activity focuses on cultic language, divine speech formulas, and the Holiness Code's promise of divine indwelling.
preamble.notable_renderings: The 'pleasing aroma' formula is systematically replaced with 'received with favor.' God's walking among Israel (26:12) becomes Shekinah presence. The lex talionis is interpreted as monetary compensation rather than literal bodily punishment. The Holiness Code's blessings and curses receive careful theological treatment.
preamble.theological_themes: Anti-anthropomorphism in sacrificial language (God does not smell); Shekinah indwelling as the goal of holiness; halakhic interpretation of legal provisions; the covenant relationship as mediated through cult and presence.

renderings.targum_text: letaqbalah bereva qodam Adonai
renderings.targum_rendering: To be received with favor before the LORD.
renderings.mt_rendering: A pleasing aroma to the LORD.
notes: This formula appears dozens of times in Leviticus. Onkelos consistently replaces 'a pleasing aroma' with 'to be received with favor' (letaqbalah bereva), removing the implication that God smells sacrifices. The shift is from sensory perception to divine acceptance.

renderings.targum_text: qurbana letaqbalah bereva qodam Adonai
renderings.targum_rendering: An offering to be received with favor before the LORD.
renderings.mt_rendering: A fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
notes: Onkelos renders 'fire offering' (issheh) as 'offering' (qurbana), and again replaces the aroma formula. The entire sensory dimension of sacrifice is reframed as a relational act of divine acceptance.

renderings.targum_text: arei yoma den Adonai itgeli lakhon
renderings.targum_rendering: For today the LORD will reveal himself to you.
renderings.mt_rendering: For today the LORD will appear to you.
notes: The promise of theophany at the Tabernacle's inauguration is rendered as revelation rather than appearance, consistent with all Onkelos theophanies.

renderings.targum_text: veyitgeli lakhon yeqara daAdonai
renderings.targum_rendering: And the glory of the LORD will be revealed to you.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the glory of the LORD will appear to you.
notes: Even when the Hebrew already specifies 'glory' (kavod) rather than God directly, Onkelos still changes 'appear' to 'be revealed.' The consistent verbal substitution reinforces that all divine manifestation is revelation, not physical appearance.

renderings.targum_text: ve'itgeli yeqara daAdonai lekhol amma
renderings.targum_rendering: And the glory of the LORD was revealed to all the people.
renderings.mt_rendering: And the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people.
notes: At the climactic moment of the Tabernacle's inaugural service, the glory is 'revealed' (itgeli) rather than 'appeared.' This is the culmination of the Shekinah theology running through Exodus into Leviticus.

renderings.targum_text: venafqat esha min qodam Adonai
renderings.targum_rendering: And fire went out from before the LORD.
renderings.mt_rendering: And fire came out from before the LORD.
notes: Onkelos preserves 'from before the LORD' (min qodam Adonai), which the Hebrew already provides (millifnei YHWH). The deaths of Nadab and Abihu are narrated with minimal interpretive adjustment.

renderings.targum_text: arei be'anana itgeli yeqari al kappureta
renderings.targum_rendering: For in the cloud my glory will be revealed upon the mercy seat.
renderings.mt_rendering: For I will appear in the cloud upon the mercy seat.
notes: God's presence above the mercy seat on Yom Kippur is rendered as the revelation of his glory (yeqar) in the cloud. The High Priest encounters the glory, not God's unmediated self.

renderings.hebrew_text: אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשֶׂה אֹתָם הָאָדָם וָחַי בָּהֶם
renderings.targum_text: di ya'avid yathon enasha veyeqayyam behon
renderings.targum_rendering: Which if a man does them, he shall live by them.
renderings.mt_rendering: Which if a man does them, he shall live by them.
notes: Onkelos renders this verse literally — a verse that becomes central to Paul's argument in Galatians 3:12 and Romans 10:5. The targum does not qualify or condition the promise of life through obedience.

renderings.hebrew_text: קְדֹשִׁים תִּהְיוּ כִּי קָדוֹשׁ אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם
renderings.targum_text: qaddishin tehevun arei qaddish ana Adonai Elahakhon
renderings.targum_rendering: You shall be holy, for I, the LORD your God, am holy.
renderings.mt_rendering: You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy.
notes: The foundational holiness command is rendered literally. Onkelos treats divine holiness as a non-anthropomorphic attribute that requires no adjustment — holiness is God's essential character, not a physical quality.

renderings.targum_text: va'ana ashavvei yat rug'zi begavra hahu
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will set my anger against that man.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will set my face against that man.
notes: 'Setting my face against' becomes 'setting my anger against.' God does not turn a physical face; he directs his wrath. The anthropomorphic face is replaced with the anthropopathic but less physical 'anger.'

renderings.targum_text: ve'ashavvei yat rug'zi benafsha hahi
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will set my anger against that soul.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will set my face against that soul.
notes: Repeated pattern: 'face' becomes 'anger' in contexts of divine judgment. Onkelos consistently applies this substitution in Leviticus' penalty clauses.

renderings.hebrew_text: אֲנִי יְהוָה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם הַמּוֹצִיא אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לִהְיוֹת לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים
renderings.targum_text: ana Adonai meqaddeshkhon de'appeiqit yathkhon me'ar'a deMitzrayim lemehevei lakhon l'Elah
renderings.targum_rendering: I am the LORD who sanctifies you, who brought you out from the land of Egypt to be your God.
renderings.mt_rendering: I am the LORD who sanctifies you, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God.
notes: The Exodus-holiness-covenant formula is rendered literally, underscoring that Onkelos treats the covenant relationship itself as requiring no intermediary language.

renderings.targum_text: lefash lehon al meimart Adonai
renderings.targum_rendering: To clarify for them according to the decree of the LORD.
renderings.mt_rendering: Until the decision of the LORD should be clear to them.
notes: 'The mouth of the LORD' (pi YHWH) becomes 'the decree/word (meimra) of the LORD,' replacing the anthropomorphic 'mouth' with the more abstract 'word/decree' — a variant of the Memra concept applied to legal decisions.

renderings.hebrew_text: שֶׁבֶר תַּחַת שֶׁבֶר עַיִן תַּחַת עַיִן שֵׁן תַּחַת שֵׁן
renderings.targum_text: demei tevirata chalaf tevirata demei eina chalaf eina demei shinna chalaf shinna
renderings.targum_rendering: The value of a fracture for a fracture, the value of an eye for an eye, the value of a tooth for a tooth.
renderings.mt_rendering: Fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth.
notes: Onkelos inserts 'the value of' (demei) before each injury, interpreting the lex talionis as monetary compensation rather than literal physical retaliation. This aligns with rabbinic halakhah in the Talmud (Bava Kamma 83b-84a).

renderings.targum_text: im beqayamai tehalkhun... ve'eittein mitreichon be'iddanehon
renderings.targum_rendering: If you walk in my statutes... I will give your rains in their season.
renderings.mt_rendering: If you walk in my statutes... I will give your rains in their season.
notes: The blessings of the Holiness Code are rendered literally, reflecting Onkelos' straightforward treatment of conditional covenant promises.

renderings.targum_text: ve'afnei Memri lakhon
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will turn my Memra toward you.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will turn toward you.
notes: God's turning toward Israel is mediated through the Memra. The covenantal blessing of divine attention operates through the Word.

renderings.targum_text: ve'eittein Shekhineti beineikhon
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will set my Shekinah among you.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will set my dwelling among you.
notes: The Hebrew mishkan ('dwelling/tabernacle') is rendered Shekinah, making explicit what the Hebrew implies: the mishkan is the locus of the Shekinah. God's 'dwelling' is his Shekinah-presence.

renderings.targum_text: ve'eittein Shekhineti beineikhon ve'ehei lakhon l'Elah
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will set my Shekinah among you, and I will be your God.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will walk among you and will be your God.
notes: God does not 'walk' among Israel. The anthropomorphic 'walking' is replaced with Shekinah indwelling. This is the covenant-presence promise in its fullest Levitical expression, and Paul cites it in 2 Corinthians 6:16.

renderings.targum_text: ve'ashavvei rug'zi bekhon
renderings.targum_rendering: And I will set my anger against you.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I will set my face against you.
notes: In the curses section, 'face' again becomes 'anger,' mirroring the pattern in Leviticus 20. The contrast is stark: in the blessings (26:9), God turns his Memra; in the curses (26:17), God sets his anger.

renderings.targum_text: vetarchiq Memri yatkhon
renderings.targum_rendering: And my Memra will distance you.
renderings.mt_rendering: And my soul will abhor you.
notes: God does not have a 'soul' (nefesh) that can 'abhor.' Onkelos replaces the anthropopathic expression with the Memra distancing Israel — divine rejection is described in relational rather than emotional terms.

renderings.hebrew_text: וְלֹא גְעַלְתִּים... לְהָפֵר בְּרִיתִי אִתָּם כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם
renderings.targum_text: vela racheiqit yathon... lebattala keyami immehon arei ana Adonai Elahehon
renderings.targum_rendering: And I did not distance them... to break my covenant with them, for I am the LORD their God.
renderings.mt_rendering: And I did not abhor them... to break my covenant with them, for I am the LORD their God.
notes: Even in the depths of the curses, God did not fully 'distance' (Memra language) Israel. The covenant endures. Onkelos renders the promise of ultimate covenant faithfulness with the same substitution pattern, maintaining theological consistency.

renderings.hebrew_text: אֵלֶּה הַחֻקִּים וְהַמִּשְׁפָּטִים וְהַתּוֹרֹת אֲשֶׁר נָתַן יְהוָה בֵּינוֹ וּבֵין בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל
renderings.targum_text: ilein qayamayya vedinayya ve'orayata di yehav Adonai bein Memreih uvein benei Yisrael
renderings.targum_rendering: These are the statutes and the judgments and the laws which the LORD gave between his Memra and the children of Israel.
renderings.mt_rendering: These are the statutes and the judgments and the laws which the LORD gave between himself and the children of Israel.
notes: The laws are given between God's Memra and Israel, not between God directly and Israel. The entire Torah is a Memra-mediated covenant — the Word stands between God and his people as the medium of revelation and relationship.
